stories.title: Pressure unlocks 3D superconductivity in tantalum disulfide at triple the temperature
stories.desc: Superconductors have long been considered a promising technology for the energy systems of the future. They can conduct electricity without resistance, thus eliminating both conduction losses and waste heat. Up to now, however, superconductors have only been applied in special cases, as in the…

stories.title: Evidence of elusive high-energy gravitons in quantum Hall systems
stories.desc: Electrons, negatively charged particles, sometimes coordinate their movements in ways that produce certain collective excitations referred to as quasiparticles. One case in which this occurs is the quantum Hall effect, a phenomenon that emerges when electrons are confined to a very thin layer,…

stories.title: Quantum computers model nine fusion fuel material configurations for first time
stories.desc: A team of scientists from Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Cleveland Clinic and IBM has calculated nine molecular configurations of a promising material to produce fuel for fusion energy—the first known instance of such computations on quantum computers.

stories.title: Quantum vacuum could help break molecular bonds with less energy, simulations suggest
stories.desc: A team of researchers led by Felipe Herrera, a professor at the University of Santiago and a researcher at the Millennium Institute for Research in Optics (MIRO), has identified a quantum phenomenon that enables chemical bonds to be broken using significantly less energy than is normally required.

stories.title: New ultrathin lens focuses light into an optical needle
stories.desc: Researchers have created a special flat lens that shapes light into an optical needle—a thin beam that stays tightly focused over a long distance. Combining this lens, which is about 7 microns thick, with optical coherence tomography (OCT) could allow imaging that reaches deeper into tissue while…

stories.title: Using quantum entanglement to secure ground-to-satellite timing
stories.desc: From mobile phones and banking systems to aircraft, ships and emergency services, much of modern life relies on precise timing signals from satellites. Known as the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS), satellites carrying atomic clocks transmit time-stamped signals to receivers on Earth. The…

stories.title: Metallic rutile oxides break the rules of cooling
stories.desc: Physicists have long puzzled over a strange contradiction inside a family of minerals called rutile oxides. These materials all share the same crystal structure—but while some of them, like titanium dioxide, are firmly insulating, others, like ruthenium dioxide, conduct electricity like a metal. So…

stories.title: Magnetic octupole model captures domain-wall motion in noncollinear antiferromagnets
stories.desc: Researchers from The Grainger College of Engineering at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign have developed the first magnetic multipole-based micromagnetic model for antiferromagnets. Published in Applied Physics Reviews, their generalized framework provides a theoretical and computational…

stories.title: Schrödinger’s anthill: Quantum entanglement found in a crystal large enough to hold
stories.desc: A centimeter-sized crystal has revealed clear signs of quantum entanglement, showing that large, everyday objects can display surprisingly deep quantum behavior. The discovery could help solve the mystery of strange metals while opening new possibilities for ultra-precise quantum sensors and other…
